title: Figure 17 — Operator Sequential Operation Diagram
source: Siemens 38kV Vacuum Circuit Breaker Type 38-3AF, Instruction Manual SG-3528, p.15 (Interrupter/Operator Description)
note: Flowchart of the closing and tripping sequences. Click any step to see what it means; device codes follow ANSI/IEEE device numbers (27 undervoltage, 52 breaker, 88 motor).

hotspots.name: Control voltage applied
hotspots.desc: Start of the closing sequence: control power is applied to the breaker's control circuit. This energizes the undervoltage device (if fitted) and the spring-charging motor.

hotspots.name: Undervoltage Device 27 picks up
hotspots.desc: Optional undervoltage device (ANSI device 27) energizes and 'picks up', arming it to trip the breaker automatically if the supply voltage later collapses. The asterisk (*) marks it as an optional item.

hotspots.name: Spring Charging Motor 88 energized
hotspots.desc: The spring-charging motor (device 88) is energized and begins charging the closing spring automatically. It runs until the closing spring is fully charged.

hotspots.name: Closing Spring Fully Charged
hotspots.desc: The closing spring reaches full charge. This throws several limit switches (LS21/LS22, LS3, LS41) that de-energize the motor and signal 'ready to close'.

hotspots.name: LS21 and LS22 open
hotspots.desc: Limit switches LS21 and LS22 open to de-energize the spring-charging motor (88) now that the spring is charged — the motor stops until the spring is next discharged.

hotspots.name: LS3 opens (anti-pump path)
hotspots.desc: Limit switch LS3, in series with the anti-pump relay 52Y, opens. LS3 is part of the interlock that prevents 'pumping' (repeated reclosing on a persistent close command).

hotspots.name: LS41 closes — springs charged signal
hotspots.desc: Limit switch LS41 closes to provide the electrical 'closing springs charged' indication/signal.

hotspots.name: No action — 52b blocks spring release
hotspots.desc: Decision branch (breaker already CLOSED): the open 52b contact — in series with the spring-release solenoid 52SRC — blocks spring release, so nothing happens. 52b is the auxiliary contact that is closed only when the breaker is open.

hotspots.name: Closing command when… (decision)
hotspots.desc: Central decision diamond. The outcome of a closing command depends on the breaker state: BREAKER CLOSED → no action; BREAKER OPEN & spring not charged → no action; BREAKER OPEN & spring charged → the spring-release solenoid fires and the breaker closes.

hotspots.name: Spring release solenoid actuated
hotspots.desc: Breaker OPEN with the closing spring charged: the spring-release solenoid is actuated through the closed 52b contact and two NC contacts of relay 52Y — releasing the stored energy to close the breaker.

hotspots.name: No action — 52Y picks up, opens
hotspots.desc: In the 'no action' branch, anti-pump relay 52Y picks up through the closed LS3 contact and then opens its own contacts — sealing out further spring release until conditions reset.

hotspots.name: The closing spring is unlatched
hotspots.desc: The closing spring is unlatched — its stored energy is now driving the mechanism to close the breaker.

hotspots.name: The tripping spring is charged
hotspots.desc: During the close stroke the mechanism also charges the tripping spring, so the breaker is immediately ready to open (enabling the open-close-open auto-reclose cycle).

hotspots.name: 52a contacts enable a trip
hotspots.desc: The 52a contacts (closed when the breaker is closed), in series with the tripping solenoid, close to enable a trip operation — the breaker can now be tripped.

hotspots.name: Auxiliary switches 52a/52b change state
hotspots.desc: As the breaker closes, the breaker auxiliary switches change state: 52a (normally-open, follows the main contacts) closes and 52b (normally-closed) opens.

hotspots.name: The circuit breaker closes
hotspots.desc: The main vacuum contacts close — the circuit breaker is now CLOSED and carrying current.

hotspots.name: LS21/LS22 re-energize motor 88
hotspots.desc: After closing, the discharged closing spring lets LS21 and LS22 close again to re-energize motor 88 and recharge the spring; LS3 closes and LS4 opens to cancel the closing-spring signal. The mechanism re-arms for the next operation.

hotspots.name: Continuous closing command
hotspots.desc: Anti-pumping scenario start: a closing command is applied and held continuously (e.g. a stuck close contact). The anti-pump feature (device 52Y) must prevent the breaker from reclosing after it trips on a fault — otherwise repeated reclosing ('pumping') can damage it.

hotspots.name: 52SRC unlatches — breaker closes
hotspots.desc: The spring-release coil 52SRC unlatches the closing spring and the breaker closes — the first close of the continuous command.

hotspots.name: Motor cutoff LS21/LS22/LS3 closed
hotspots.desc: Because the closing spring is now discharged, the motor-cutoff limit switches LS21, LS22 and LS3 are closed — restoring the motor circuit so the spring can recharge.

hotspots.name: 52Y picks up and seals in
hotspots.desc: Before motor 88 has recharged the closing spring and reopened LS3, the anti-pump relay 52Y picks up and 'seals in' (latches itself electrically) — this is the key anti-pump action.

hotspots.name: 52Y opens contacts to 52SRC
hotspots.desc: The energized anti-pump relay 52Y opens two of its contacts that are in series with the spring-release coil 52SRC — cutting the path that would otherwise fire another close.

hotspots.name: Spring release blocked
hotspots.desc: The spring-release solenoid is now blocked and cannot be activated until the springs are fully charged AND the close command has been removed — preventing repeated reclosing on a held command.

hotspots.name: Trip Command
hotspots.desc: Start of the tripping sequence. A trip can be commanded by the local trip button, the shunt trip coil 52T, the undervoltage device 27, or an optional secondary (dual-trip) release.

hotspots.name: Shunt Trip Coil 52T (breaker closed)
hotspots.desc: The shunt trip coil (device 52T) can only be activated when the series-connected 52a contact is closed — i.e. only when the breaker is actually closed, preventing a wasted trip pulse on an open breaker.

hotspots.name: Undervoltage 27 — NC contact opens
hotspots.desc: Optional undervoltage trip path: device 27 is activated by opening a normally-closed contact in series with 27, or by loss/reduction of the tripping voltage itself. (* optional)

hotspots.name: Undervoltage 27 — NO contact shorts coil
hotspots.desc: Alternative undervoltage path: device 27 is activated by closing a normally-open contact that shorts the 27 coil. The NO contact is connected across 27 by the 52a contact, so it is only effective while the breaker is closed. (* optional)

hotspots.name: Secondary release (dual trip)
hotspots.desc: Optional secondary release providing a dual-trip function, activated by a remote trip-command normally-open contact — used when a second, independent trip path is required. (* optional)

hotspots.name: 52T unlatches tripping spring
hotspots.desc: The shunt trip coil 52T unlatches the tripping spring, releasing the stored energy that opens the breaker.

hotspots.name: 27 unlatches tripping spring
hotspots.desc: The undervoltage device (27) unlatches the tripping spring — the optional automatic path that opens the breaker on loss of voltage. (* optional)

hotspots.name: Secondary release unlatches spring
hotspots.desc: The optional secondary release unlatches the tripping spring via its independent trip path. (* optional)

hotspots.name: Circuit breaker trips
hotspots.desc: End state: the tripping spring drives the mechanism open and the circuit breaker trips (opens its main contacts).
